entry.title: 客户项目首付款开票流程
entry.content:
本博文简单介绍Professional Service客户项目首付款开票流程

系统演示

1.创建客户项目，建立开票，案例用的是固定价格合同开票类型

合同总金额3000，首付款1000

2.APP：管理项目开票，点击黄色三角形查看预付款金额

点击设置按钮，把字段Down Payment Received和Down Payment Requested勾选。

选择开票项，点击Prepayment

点击Submit

开票请求已生成

APP:创建开票凭证，设置开票类型FAZ，对首付款进行开票并过账生成日记账

点击Entry View查看分录

3. APP: Post Incoming Payment

查看日记账分录

4.创建总发票，APP:管理项目开票，点击Prepare Billing

开票凭证请求已生成

APP:创建开票凭证，设置开票类型CI01

查看日记账分录，冲销了首付款的分录

感谢观看

entry.title: Strengthen Your SAP Projects in Q2 with SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council
entry.content:
Looking for more hands-on support and early access to the latest SAP Enterprise Support services?

With SAP Enterprise Support, part of the Foundational Success Plan included in all cloud subscriptions, you already have a strong foundation to get up and running, safeguard business continuity, and drive ongoing value.

Through the SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC), you can go even further. As a key component of this offering, ESAC connects you directly with SAP experts and peers to bring hands-on support to your projects, co-innovate, test new services early, and help shape future support offerings.

Why join?

  • Get expert guidance for your projects
  • Access and influence new services before general release
  • Turn your feedback into real support improvements

What’s New in Q2?

New workstreams:

  • CQC* Transformation Assessment for SAP Transportation Management (Blog)
  • CQC Transformation Assessment for SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Blog)

Still open to new customers:

  • CQC Technical Preparation for SAP Signavio Process Insights and Intelligence (Blog)
  • CQC Deployment Readiness for the Integrated Toolchain (Blog)
  • CQC Deployment Readiness for SAP Signavio Process Manager and Collaboration Hub (Blog)
  • CQC Security Optimization for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Blog)
  • EGI* Support Migration to SAP Integration Suite (Blog)

Discover our workstreams: workstream overviews

Get Involved? 

  • Learn more: visit us on SAP Support Portal. 
  • contact us:  esac@sap.com

Your SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council team

*Continuous Quality Check (CQC): Work with our experts to analyze your system using real data and receive a report with key findings and a clear action plan to improve outcomes and reduce risks.

*Expert-Guided Implementation (EGI): A multi-day, remote, workshop-style session where participants learn to complete a defined activity with the support of experienced SAP service engineers.

entry.title: BAdi Enhancement Implementation using Developer Extensibility in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

In On Stack extensibility, Enhancements can be created using BAdi Enhancement Implementation in Developer Extensibility.

Developer extensibility using SAP Cloud ABAP Environment for developer extensions.

 

Business Accelerator Hub

SAP Business Accelerator Hub provides a section for On Stack extensibility (key user extensibility and developer extensibility), to know list of SAP CDS views, APIs, Business Add-ins etc. are available with the release status.

On Stack Extensibility -> Business Add-ins

Search for required BAdi.

This BAdi is related to check purchase order before saving to create it.

Here it shows BAdi Definition and Enhancement spot.

BAdi can be used in both Key User and Developer Extensibility as both states of BAdi is in released state.

Methods and its Parameters

Developer Extensibility – BAdi Enhancement Implementation

Steps to Create Impelementation

Create New BAdi Enhancement Implementation

Chose BAdi Enhancement Implementation.

Add Name and description.

Add Enhancement Spot

BAdi Enhancement Implementation is created.

Choose BAdi Implementation to add Implementations.

Add BAdi Definition and Custom Class name

Add Implementation Class

Choose Implementation Class, add Custom Class name and its description

Implementation Class is created.

 

ZCL_CHECK_PO▸
CLASS zcl_check_po DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
   
   INTERFACES if_badi_interface
   INTERFACES if_ex_mmpur_final_check_po .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_check_po IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_ex_mmpur_final_check_po~check. 
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

Implementation Class is added to Badi Implementation details

Logic can be added to implementation class

 

ZCL_CHECK_PO▸
CLASS zcl_check_po DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
   
   INTERFACES if_badi_interface
   INTERFACES if_ex_mmpur_final_check_po .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_check_po IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_ex_mmpur_final_check_po~check. 
    IF purchaseorder-supplier EQ '0000100005'. 
      APPEND VALUE #( messagetype = 'E' messagevariable1= |Choose another Supplier] ) TO messages.
    ENDIF.  
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

Navigate to Create Purchase Order (ME21N)

Provide the required details and check

Error appeared as per logic written.

Add breakpoint in the implementation class to debug.

Place cursor on the logic, choose right click.

Break point is added

Break point can be added in the logic, and it triggers when creating purchase order in the Create Purchase Order (ME21N).

Conclusion

In Developer Extensibility, BAdi Enhancement Implementation can be created and deleted.

Custom Implementation can be captured in TR's and those can be moved to test, production systems.

This custom implementation can also be debugged.

References

  • Extensibility | SAP Help Portal
  • Developer Extensibility | SAP Help Portal
  • Implementing BAdI Enhancement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Setting Mandatory Fields in Purchase Requisitions via Custom Logic in Public Cloud
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, Purchase Requisitions (PRs) often require additional field validations to ensure data completeness and process compliance. While some fields can be defined as mandatory through standard customizing, this is limited to predefined fields and specific scenarios.

This blog post explains how to set additional standard fields as mandatory in Purchase Requisitions by using Custom Logic with Key User Extensibility.

The solution applies to the following apps:

  • Manage Purchase Requisitions – Professional
  • Create Purchase Requisition – Advanced

Business Requirement

The business requirement was to enforce mandatory entry of the following fields at purchase requisition item level:

  • Purchasing Organization (Field EKORG)
  • Requisitioner (Field AFNAM)

Why standard customizing is not sufficient

Using the customizing activity Maintain Account Assignment Categories (ID 101815), some fields (for example Cost Center) can be set as mandatory. However:

  • This applies only during the creation of an account assignment category
  • Only predefined fields are available
  • Additional standard fields cannot be enforced

To cover these gaps, Custom Logic (BAdIs) must be used.

Solution Overview

To fulfill the requirements, two different Custom Logic extension points are used:

Both extension points are available via the Custom Logic app in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Custom Logic 1: Change of Field Control (Purchasing Organization)

Extension Point: Change of Field Control for Purchase Requisitions

Extension Point ID: MM_PUR_S4_PR_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE

Description

This BAdI allows you to:

  • Hide fields
  • Set fields as read-only
  • Set fields as mandatory
  • Set fields as optional

It works directly at UI field control level.

⚠️Limitation
This extension point supports the field PURREQNSSPREQUESTOR, which is relevant only for Self‑Service Procurement.
It does not enforce the Requisitioner field in apps Manage Purchase Requisitions - Professional or Create Purchase Requisition - Advanced.

Sample Code –  Change of Field Control (Purchasing Organization) 

* Sample Code

    FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs> TYPE mmpur_s_pr_fldsel.


   READ TABLE fieldselection_table ASSIGNING <fs> WITH KEY field = 'PURCHASINGORGANIZATION'.
    IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
     <fs>-fieldstatus = '+'.
    ENDIF.

* END OF Sample Code
 
✔️  Result:
  • The Purchasing Organization field becomes mandatory on PR item level.

Custom Logic 2: Check of Purchase Requisition Item (Requisitioner)

Since the Requisitioner (AFNAM) cannot be enforced via field control, a check-based validation is required.

Extension Point: Check of Purchase Requisition Item

Extension Point ID: MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK

Description

This BAdI allows you to:

  • Perform custom validations
  • Trigger error messages
  • Prevent saving the Purchase Requisition

Sample Code – Check of Purchase Requisition Item (Requisitioner)

* Sample Code

DATA: ls_message LIKE LINE OF messages.

IF PURCHASEREQUISITION-PURCHASINGDOCUMENTTYPE = 'NB'.
LOOP AT purchaserequisitionitem_table INTO DATA(ls_item).
      IF ls_item-PURREQNREQUESTOR = ''.

         APPEND VALUE #( messagetype = 'E'
         messageid = 'ME'
         messagenumber = '999'
         messagevariable1 = 'Enter Requisitioner / Anforderer fehlt' ) TO messages.
         purchaserequisitionhaserror = abap_true.
         RETURN.
      ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.

* END OF Sample Code

 

✔️  Result:
  • The PR cannot be saved if the Requisitioner is missing
  • A clear error message is displayed to the user

Transport

Custom Logic implementations are not transported automatically and must be added to a Software Collection.

Steps

  1. Open Export Software Collection
  2. Select an existing collection or create a new one
  3. Choose Add Items
  4. Filter by:
    • Last Changed On
    • Last Changed By
  5. Add the Custom Logic entries
  6. Transport the collection as usual

Summary

By combining two Custom Logic extension points, it is possible to:

  • Enforce mandatory fields beyond standard customizing limits
  • Apply validations consistently across professional and advanced PR apps
  • Stay fully within SAP-recommended extensibility

This approach provides a flexible and upgrade-safe way to enhance Purchase Requisition quality.

References

  • 3345421 - BAdI: Field Control for Purchase Requisitions - SAP for Me
  • How to Manage and Export Your Software Collection | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Instructor‑Led Training Now Available: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance
entry.content:
Following our recent post announcing the new Learning Journey Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance, we would like to remind you that the corresponding instructor‑led Training is already open for registration and coming up soon.

SAP Business AI is shaping how Finance processes are designed and executed today. While the Learning Journey offers flexible, self‑paced access to this topic, the instructor‑led Training provides an opportunity to deepen this knowledge in a guided, hands‑on setting with direct expert interaction.

Instructor‑led Training: Applying Business AI in SAP Finance

The two‑day instructor‑led Training focuses on the practical application of SAP Business AI in Finance and is designed for consultants and finance professionals who want to actively work with these capabilities.

During the training, participants will:

  • Apply SAP Joule in concrete Finance processes and scenarios
  • Work hands‑on with SAP Joule Agents to support more complex Finance activities
  • Explore consultant‑focused SAP Joule use cases for analysis, explanation, and solution design
  • Gain practical insights into Finance functionality within SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Understand the technical and functional foundation needed to position and demonstrate AI in SAP Finance

The course places strong emphasis on interactive exercises, live demonstrations, and real‑world Finance examples, allowing participants to directly transfer what they learn into project and customer contexts.

Instructor‑led Training details:

  • Duration: 2 full days
  • Delivery format: Hybrid, with on‑site participation in Walldorf and virtual attendance
  • Upcoming session: June 10–11
  • Language: German for this session; English sessions are planned and will be announced

Registration link: F0610 - Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance | SAP Training

You’re also welcome to check out our new Business AI Learning Journey: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance
 
Both the Learning Journey and the instructor‑led Training stand on their own and do not require completion of the other. They can be taken individually or combined to deepen Business AI expertise in SAP Finance.
Both formats support the development of practical, role‑relevant AI expertise in SAP Finance.

We look forward to your participation.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting: Using Automatic Postings for Revenue-Based Cost Distribution
entry.content:
Introduction

In Group Reporting, the financial "Close" process is frequently delayed by the reliance on external distribution cycles. Distributing shared costs or risks across entities often requires moving data between modules, creating silos, and slowing down the path to a consolidated view.

By utilizing Automatic Postings, organizations can implement simple logic directly within the consolidation engine. This native approach offers a transparent, audit-ready method for managing financial adjustments. This post shows how to automate the distribution of Bad Debt Allowance to profit centers based on Revenue drivers, ensuring your consolidated data reflects real-world credit exposure with clinical precision.

The Scenario: Revenue-Based Distribution

In this scenario, our objective is to distribute Bad Debt Allowance (FS Item 583000) across Profit Centers. The distribution is proportional to the Revenue (FS Item 411100 – Sale of Goods) generated by each profit center.

The underlying financial logic is straightforward: Higher Revenue → Higher Credit Exposure → Higher Bad Debt Allowance.

By automating this with the rules for automatic postings, we ensure that as revenue figures shift, the corresponding risk provision is redistributed without manual intervention or data.

Source Data (Before Distribution)

Results (After Distribution)

Solution Overview: The Modular Rule Design

To achieve this distribution, we employ a modular two-rule design. This structure allows the system first to establish the driver and then apply it to the financial values.

Rule ID

Purpose

Outcome

Rule 1

Calculate Revenue Share (%)

Calculates the percentage of total revenue per Profit Center and stores it as a quantity driver (%).

Rule 2

Distribute Bad Debt

Consumes the stored quantity driver (%) to post the proportional bad debt distribution.

Step 1: Calculating the Revenue Share (The Driver)

Rule 1 identifies the revenue for each Profit Center and determines its share relative to the total.

First, we define a selection, Z_SOG, to filter relevant source data. The criteria include:

  • FS Item: 411100 (Sale of Goods)
  • Posting levels: #, 0C, 00, 01
  • Profit center: not empty

Selection used in Rule 1:

Posting Logic & Variables To ensure the calculation is dynamic, we use the variable Z_VAR_SOG to represent the total revenue across all relevant Profit Centers. The system then calculates the share for each specific Profit Center using the following formula:

Revenue Share per profit center = SOURCE_LC / Z_VAR_SOG

Variable used in Rule 1:

Since the driver calculation must be processed within each consolidation unit, it is important to include the field Consolidation Unit in the list of matching fields of the variable.

Rule 1 Setting:

Record the result of this calculation to a statistical FS item, such as ZS_PC_SHR, and set the Base Unit to PRC (Percentage). This means that the FS item must be assigned to a breakdown category with unit of measure. Also, it is critical to enable "Posting with Quantities." Setting the unit to PRC ensures that a result of "0.15" is correctly interpreted as 15% during the multiplication in Step 2.

Step 2: Performing the Bad Debt Distribution

Once Rule 1 has prepared the drivers and stored them as quantities, Rule 2 executes the actual distribution. Rule 2 requires a Variable Z_VAR_BDA to select the total Bad Debt Allowance Amount.

Variable used in Rule 2:

Here again, it is important to include the field Consolidation Unit in the list of matching fields of the variable because the distribution must be processed within each consolidation unit.

The system applies the following formula to distribute the bad debt value across the target Profit Centers:

Distributed Value = Bad Debt * Z_PC_SHARE (Profit Center Share)

Selection used in Rule 2:

Rule 2 Setting:

The first posting line records the distributed amount on each profit center, and the second posting line reverses the full amount initially reported without profit center. Using the formula Balance() ensure that not rounding entry will remain.

 

Rule Group Setting:

The Rule Group includes above two rules, which are designed to work together in sequence.

The first rule prepares the driver, which is calculated based on the sales revenue for each profit center. This step is essential because it creates the weighting factor that will be used in the next step.

The second rule then uses this driver to distribute the bad debt across the profit centers. Since this distribution relies directly on the values calculated in the first rule, there is a clear dependency between the two.

To ensure everything runs correctly, the dependency is explicitly defined in the rule group by setting the second rule to “Depends on Previous Rules” . This guarantees that the first rule is executed before the second one. The sequence number further reflects this order, making sure the driver is prepared first, so the bad debt distribution can be calculated accurately based on it.

The result is a proportional distribution that accurately aligns risk provisions with revenue performance, visible in the task log .

Key Design Decisions & Why They Matter

  • Quantities as Drivers: By storing percentages as quantities, once a driver is calculated for a period, it remains in the database as a quantity that can be referenced by multiple rules without re-calculation.
  • Variable Usage (Z_VAR_SOG): Utilizing variables for the "Total" denominator ensures the calculation remains dynamic. If a new Profit Center is added or revenue data is updated, the variable automatically reflects the new total, ensuring the sum of shares always equals 100%.
  • Auditability via Document Types: We recommend dedicated Document Types (e.g., Z7, Z8) for these distributions. This isolates the distribution logic from original reported data, providing a clear trail for auditors to distinguish between original balances and calculated results.

Conclusion & Community Engagement

Working with the “Manage Rules for Automatic Postings” app is less technical than it may initially appear. In practice, defining simple distribution rules is quick and requires minimal effort—especially compared to other solutions that rely on scripting or complex calculation logic. A key advantage is how easily these basic setups can be extended to support more dynamic distribution scenarios, without introducing additional complexity. From a group reporting consultant’s perspective, this simplicity streamlines implementation and keeps teams flexible, while ensuring full audit transparency.

 

References:

SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting - Introducing New Rules for Automatic Postings 

SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting - Using Variables and Functions in Rules for Automatic Postings   

Rules for Automatic Postings 

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Special thanks to @GregoireDesombrefor his thoughtful review of this blog—much appreciated!

entry.title: Managing Multi-Country/Region Implementations with a Parallel Line
entry.content:
Co-authored by @StefanBarth  and @Nicola_Pace 

Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition across multiple countries/regions is a complex endeavor that requires careful planning and robust configuration management. The parallel line provides a powerful solution for managing multi-wave, multi-country/region rollouts without disrupting your productive environment. In this post, I’ll walk you through a proven approach for leveraging the parallel line to run phased country/region implementations efficiently and safely.

For more information about the system landscape with a parallel line, parallel line management, and the lifecycle processes branch, rebase, and merge, refer to the product assistance on SAP Help Portal.

Why Use a Parallel Line for Multi-Country/Region Implementations?

The parallel line offers a separate workspace in SAP Central Business Configuration and additional SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenants in your development and test systems. This separation enables you to:

  • Develop and test new country/region configurations without affecting your stable main line
  • Maintain operational continuity in live countries/regions while preparing for future waves
  • Validate complex configurations thoroughly before merging them into your main line
  • Reduce risk by isolating configuration changes until they're fully tested

Understanding the Foundation: The 5-Country/Region Limit

SAP Central Business Configuration has a critical deployment constraint that directly impacts how you plan and run multi-country/region implementations. The 5-country/region limit is a technical recommendation designed to optimize system performance and ensure smooth activation processes in the main line as well as the parallel line.

When the Limit Applies

This limit applies every time there is deployment and activation from SAP Central Business Configuration to the customizing tenant of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • For each group or batch of countries/regions implemented in the main line and deployed from the SAP Central Business Configuration workspace to the customizing tenant (D.100) in the main line
  • For each group or batch of countries/regions implemented in the parallel line and deployed from the SAP Central Business Configuration workspace to the parallel customizing tenant (D.120) in the parallel line

Critically for merges: When merging from the parallel line to the main line, deployment and activation are performed in one step. In this case, 5 is the maximum number for the entire wave, not just for a single configuration group or batch

This deliberate technical limit is designed to prevent performance bottlenecks, maintain system responsiveness, and ensure smooth configuration processes during critical implementation milestones.

How the Limit Works with the Merge Strategy

When you plan to merge configurations from the parallel line to the main line, you can scope a maximum of 5 countries/regions total in the parallel line before deploying them from the SAP Central Business Configuration workspace to the parallel customizing tenant (D.120).

Key Understanding: The 5-country/region limit becomes a total limit for the entire wave when you plan to merge. This is not just a per-batch limit. It’s the maximum you can merge from the parallel line to the main line in one implementation wave.

Why the 5-Country/Region Limit Matters

The recommended batch of 5 countries/regions serves three critical purposes that directly impact your implementation success:

  • Content Deployment Optimization: Large country/region volumes can strain content activation and transport processes, potentially causing failures or excessive processing times that disrupt project timelines. By limiting batches to 5 countries/regions, you ensure that the activation process completes reliably and within acceptable timeframes.
  • System Performance: Processing too many countries/regions simultaneously can create bottlenecks that affect system responsiveness during deployment and potentially impact other users working in the system. The 5-country/region limit prevents these performance degradations, ensuring consistent system behavior throughout your implementation.
  • Configuration Efficiency: Smooth processing during scope confirmation milestones reduces project risks by making each deployment cycle predictable and manageable. When deployments consistently succeed without performance issues, your project team can focus on configuration quality rather than troubleshooting technical problems.

Critical constraint: The limit becomes absolute during merge operations, where all parallel line configurations must be processed in a single wave.

Implementing the Multi-Wave Approach

The parallel line approach allows teams to work on new country/region configurations without disrupting live operations or ongoing customer adaptations in the main line. Here’s how to implement this strategy effectively:

Wave 1: Initial Implementation in the Main Line

Start by implementing your first group of countries/regions directly in the main line. Complete all configuration, testing, and validation activities, then proceed with your wave 1 go-live. This establishes your baseline configuration and validates your core setup.

Wave 2: Branch the Main Line and Configure in the Parallel Line

Step 1: Branch the Main Line to Set Up the Parallel Line

After wave 1 is live and stable, branch the parallel line through SAP for Me. This process replicates the main line business configuration comprehensively. It includes all business configuration data, organizational structures, and reference content in the parallel line workspace.

During branching, when you enable the Copy Master Data for Business Configuration Consistency option, not only configuration master data but an extensive list of configuration and master data tables are transferred to ensure consistency. This comprehensive data transfer is critical for maintaining configuration integrity across both lines. Be aware that business users are copied without business role assignments, so you’ll need to assign business roles after branching. Also note that the development and test systems in the main line will be temporarily locked during the branch process.

Step 2: Configure Wave 2 Countries/Regions in the Parallel Line

In your parallel line, configure the second group of countries/regions (wave 2). Limit this to a maximum of 5 countries/regions to avoid performance degradation and activation issues during the subsequent merge.

In SAP Central Business Configuration, scope the new countries/regions, configure organizational structures such as company codes, plants, and sales areas, and set up country/region-specific customization and localizations. Utilize the Configuration Localization Tool (CLT) if applicable for your implementation.

During wave 2 configuration in the parallel line, limit main line activities to minor adjustments and customer adaptations only. Avoid large configuration changes in the main line to prevent complex merge conflicts later. This discipline is critical for maintaining a clean separation between lines.

Step 3: Test and Validate in the Parallel Line

Transport your configuration from the parallel customizing tenant (D.120) to the parallel test tenant (T.120) using the Export Customizing Transports and Import Collection apps. Conduct thorough system integration testing (SIT), user acceptance testing (UAT), and end-to-end business process validation.

Use the test automation tool in your parallel test tenant to streamline testing activities. For more information, refer to Testing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Step 4: Rebase from Main Line

Before merging wave 2 into the main line, perform a rebase to synchronize any incremental configuration changes made in the main line since the branch or last rebase. Regular rebasing ensures your parallel line includes urgent fixes, legal updates, or small adjustments made in your productive main line.

In SAP Central Business Configuration:

  • Initiate a rebase from the workspace for the parallel line.
  • Review possible scoping or configuration conflicts. For more information, refer to Conflict Resolution.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • Review and activate the Customer staging entries in the Display Business Configuration Stagings app.
  • Resolve any conflicts using the compare & adjust tool in the Implementation Activities app.

Step 5: Run Pre-Merge Validation

Before initiating a merge, run the Simulation of Cross-Client Validation check in the Manage Check Runs for Business Configuration app. For more information, refer to Simulation of Cross-Client Validation.

Step 6: Merge Wave 2 to Main Line

Once validation is clean and the rebase is complete, initiate a merge from the SAP Central Business Configuration workspace in the parallel line. All wave 2 configurations, including organizational structures and related product configuration, are transferred to the SAP Central Business Configuration workspace of the main line, deployed to the customizing tenant (D.100), and immediately activated there.

The merge process includes:

  • SAP Central Business Configuration staging entries
  • Customer staging entries

Activate the Customer staging entries to complete the transfer to the main line. For more information, refer to Maintain Business Configuration Stagings.

Step 7: Verify Deployment and Activation in the Customizing Tenant (D.100)

Check that the wave 2 configuration has been successfully deployed from the SAP Central Business Configuration workspace for the main line to the customizing tenant (D.100) of the development system in the main line and activated there. Review the staging status in the Display Business Configuration Stagings app.

Step 8: Transport to Test System

Move the wave 2 configuration from the customizing tenant (D.100) to the test tenant (T.100) for comprehensive testing before forwarding it to production.

Step 9: Adaptations in the Main Line

Perform any necessary product configuration for wave 2 directly in the customizing tenant (D.100). Transport these refinements to the test tenant (T.100) for final validation. This ensures that all final adjustments are captured in your production path.

Step 10: Deploy to Production

At this stage, you have two options:

  • Immediate Go-Live: If wave 2 is ready for production, transport the full configuration to the production tenant (P.100) for cutover and go-live activities.
  • Consolidated Go-Live: Keep the validated wave 2 configuration on hold in the test tenant T.100 and merge it with subsequent waves for consolidated testing before moving to production. This provides flexibility to go live with multiple waves together rather than deploying each wave individually.

Wave 3 and Beyond: Repeat the Cycle

When you’re ready for wave 3 and beyond, you have two options depending on your organizational preferences and project requirements:

 

Aspect

Option A: Clean Slate Approach

Option B: Continuous Extension

Process

Delete the existing parallel line and request to branch from the main line again

Continue using the same parallel line

Configuration

Set up a fresh parallel line and configure the next 5 countries/regions

Add up to 5 more countries/regions to the existing parallel line

Best For

Large-scale implementations requiring strict governance

Smaller rollouts where team familiarity with existing structure adds efficiency

Advantages

Cleaner tenant environment, simplified governance, reduced complexity

Continuity, efficiency if team knows the existing structure

Recommended For

Implementations with many waves (for example, 40+ countries/regions)

Short-term extensions with limited additional waves

The choice between these options depends on your preference for tenant cleanliness, organizational complexity, and the total number of remaining countries/regions to implement.

Example: Implementing 40 Countries Using the Merge Strategy

To illustrate how the 5-country/region limit works in practice across a large-scale implementation, consider a scenario where you need to implement SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition across 40 countries/regions. Using the merge strategy, you would structure your implementation as follows:

  • Wave 1: Foundation (Countries 1-5)
    Implement the first 5 countries/regions directly in the main line. Complete configuration, testing, and validation. You can either go live with these countries immediately as your baseline or keep them in the test system for consolidated deployment with subsequent waves.
  • Wave 2: First Parallel Line Use (Countries 6-10)
    After wave 1 is stable, branch the main line and configure the next 5 countries/regions in the parallel line. Test thoroughly in the parallel test tenant (T.120,) rebase to synchronize with any main line changes, then merge back to the main line. After successful testing in test tenant (T.100), decide whether to deploy to the production tenant (P.100) immediately or consolidate with additional waves before go-live.
  • Wave 3: Clean Slate (Countries 11-15)
    Delete the existing parallel line and branch again from the main line (which now contains 10 countries/regions). Configure the next 5 countries/regions in the fresh parallel line, then follow the same rebase, merge, and test process.
  • Waves 4-8: Repeat the Cycle (Countries 16-40)
    Continue with this pattern: delete the parallel line after each successful merge, branch again, configure the next batch of 5 countries/regions, and merge. You maintain flexibility to go live with selected wave groupings rather than deploying each wave individually to production. Each wave builds on the consolidated configuration in the main line, ensuring a single source of truth while respecting the 5-country/region merge limit.

Timeline Considerations

When planning your multi-wave implementation, keep the following timing factors in mind:

  • Each wave requires time for branching (8-10 hours typical), configuration, testing, rebase, merge, and deployment
  • Plan for adequate time between waves to ensure stability and allow for thorough validation
  • The clean slate approach (Option A from Wave 3 onwards) is recommended for large-scale implementations to maintain system cleanliness and reduce complexity

This systematic approach allows you to scale to any number of countries/regions while maintaining the integrity and performance benefits that the 5-country/region limit provides.

Best Practices for Success

Several key practices will significantly improve your success with parallel line implementations:

  • Sequential Wave Implementation: After completing one wave of up to 5 countries/regions, you can immediately begin planning and configuring the next wave. There’s no waiting period required between waves, which means you can maintain project momentum while respecting the technical constraints.
  • Main Line Discipline: During parallel line configuration periods, keep main line changes minimal to simplify rebase and merge operations. This discipline prevents configuration conflicts and makes synchronization between lines much smoother. Reserve your main line for urgent fixes and critical updates only while major implementation work happens in the parallel line.
  • Thorough Testing: Always validate configurations in the test tenant (T.100) or parallel test tenant (T.120) before moving to production. Never skip testing phases, even when you’re confident in your configuration. The test phase catches issues before they impact production and provides a safe environment for end-to-end process validation.
  • Regular Rebasing: Before every merge, perform a rebase to ensure your parallel line includes the latest main line updates. This practice minimizes merge conflicts and ensures consistency between your configuration tracks.
  • Clear Wave Planning: Define your country/region groupings upfront to optimize the parallel line lifecycle. Understanding which countries/regions will be implemented together, and in what sequence, allows you to plan resources, manage stakeholder expectations, and structure your project timeline realistically. Remember that the 5-country/region limit is absolute for merge operations, so plan your waves accordingly.

Conclusion

The merge strategy provides centralized governance and a single source of truth for your global implementation while leveraging the isolation and safety that the parallel line provides during configuration and testing. This transforms complex multi-country/region rollouts from overwhelming challenges into manageable, systematic processes that can be repeated reliably across multiple implementation waves.

To execute this strategy successfully, focus on the following critical practices:

  • Understanding the 5-country/region deployment limit as a strict per-wave constraint when merging configurations back to the main line
  • Branching strategically to create isolated workspaces for new country/region groups
  • Configuring efficiently within the 5-country/region limit to optimize performance
  • Rebasing regularly to stay synchronized with main line updates
  • Merging carefully after thorough validation to maintain configuration integrity
  • Minimizing main line changes during parallel line configuration periods
  • Defining country/region groupings upfront to plan resources and timelines realistically
  • Validating thoroughly in the parallel test tenant (T.120) before merging and again in the test tenant (T.100) before production deployment

By following these practices and respecting the technical constraints, organizations can scale globally while maintaining system performance and configuration quality.

Have you used the parallel line for multi-country/region implementations? I’d love to hear about your experiences and any lessons learned in the comments below!

entry.title: Advanced Success Plan im Detail - Mehr Wert aus SAP-Cloud-Investitionen ziehen
entry.content:
Im zweiten Teil der Webcast-Serie im April 2026 zu den SAP Success Plans stellte Jesper Waaben, Chief Product Owner für die Advanced Success Plans im Bereich ERP, den Advanced SuccessPlan exemplarisch vor. Während der erste Webcast den Gesamtüberblick lieferte, ging es hier um konkrete Services, neue Formate wie Success Packs und die Frage, was sich gegenüber dem Vorgänger Preferred Success tatsächlich geändert hat. Anbei die Zusammenfassung, sowie die Folien und das deutsche  Recording.  

Was bleibt, was ist neu im Advanced Success Plan von SAP?

Waaben machte transparent, wo der Advanced Success Plan auf Bewährtem aufbaut und wo er Neuland betritt.

Was bleibt:

Die proaktive Partnerschaft, die technische und funktionale Unterstützung sowie die Release Guidance. Kunden, die Preferred Success kennen, werden viele Elemente wiedererkennen.

Was neu ist:

  • Hands-on Activation: Der wohl greifbarste Unterschied. Unter Preferred Success durften SAP-Experten nicht in die Kundensysteme eingreifen – alles war „hands off". Jetzt können sie neue Funktionalitäten direkt im System aktivieren und eine erste, leichte Konfiguration anleiten. Das muss natürlich mit dem Projektteam und gegebenenfalls dem Implementierungspartner abgestimmt werden.

  • KI-gestützte Guidance: Eine in SAP Cloud ALM integrierte Lösung analysiert automatisch Systemdaten – insbesondere im Bereich SAP S/4HANA Private und Public Cloud – und leitet daraus Empfehlungen für Prozessverbesserungen, Funktionalitäten und Systemlandschaften ab.

  • Abdeckung der gesamten Business Suite: Der Advanced Plan deckt nun End-to-End-Szenarien über die gesamte SAP-Lösungslandschaft ab.

  • Verbesserte SLAs: 50 % schnellere Initial Response Times und beschleunigte Corrective Actions gegenüber dem Foundational Plan.

Die fünf Säulen des Advanced Success Plans

Der Advanced Plan ergänzt den Foundational Success Plan um fünf konkrete Bereiche:

1. Innovation and Adoption Guidance

SAP-Experten analysieren gemeinsam mit dem Customer Success Manager spezifische Funktionalitäten und Prozesse in der jeweiligen Lösung – auch funktionalitätsübergreifend. Daraus entstehen Empfehlungen: Welche Funktionalitäten lassen sich verbessern? Welche neuen Features sollten aktiviert werden?

2. Activation and Optimization Services

Hier werden die identifizierten Empfehlungen umgesetzt. SAP-Berater gehen ins System, aktivieren Funktionalitäten und leiten erste Konfigurationsschritte an. Waaben betonte: Die eingesetzten Experten sind erfahrene SAP-Berater aus dem Projektgeschäft – keine reine Support-Organisation.

3. KI-gestützte Qualitäts- und Prozess-Guidance

Die in Cloud ALM integrierte KI-Lösung nutzt gesammelte Systemdaten, um Erkenntnisse und Empfehlungen zu generieren. Voraussetzung: Cloud ALM muss aktiv genutzt werden, um den vollen Mehrwert zu realisieren.

4. Orchestrierung des Success Plans

Ein Punkt, den Waaben als häufig unterschätzt bezeichnete. Customer Success Manager und Success Plan Manager sorgen dafür, dass die richtigen Services zum richtigen Zeitpunkt eingesetzt werden. Das Ziel: weg vom Gießkannenprinzip, hin zu gezieltem Service-Einsatz. Die Orchestratoren können zu jedem Thema in einer Lösung die passenden SAP-Experten hinzuziehen.

5. Enhanced Support SLAs

Konkret: 50 % schnellere Initial Response Times und beschleunigte Corrective Actions – für Kunden mit geschäftskritischen Systemen ein relevanter Faktor. 

Abbildung: Gegenüberstellung des Advanced & Foundational Success Plan

Service Maps und Success Packs: Komplexität beherrschbar machen

Service Maps pro Lösung

Für jede SAP-Lösung – ob S/4HANA Public, S/4HANA Private, Supply Chain, Ariba, SuccessFactors, CX oder Business Data Cloud – gibt es eine eigene Service Map. Diese zeigt, welche Services konkret verfügbar sind: von fokussierten funktionalen Services bis hin zu transformativen Formaten wie dem Cloud Mindset Workshop.

Über alle Lösungen hinweg stehen die sogenannten Success Expert Services zur Verfügung:

  • Value Management: Aufbau einer Baseline für Wertverfolgung über den gesamten Lösungs-Lifecycle
  • Service Planning: Workshop-basierte Zuordnung der Kundensituation zu passenden Services, daraus entsteht ein konkreter Serviceplan
  • Adoption Checkpoint: Experten prüfen die Lösung auf ungenutztes Potenzial und neue Funktionalitäten
  • Innovation Checkpoint: Bewertung neuer Releases und Innovationen – einschließlich KI-Funktionen – für den konkreten Kundenkontext

Success Packs: Thematische Bündelung

Mit wachsendem Service-Volumen steigt die Komplexität. SAP begegnet dem mit Success Packs – thematischen Bündelungen zusammengehöriger Services. Beispiele:

  • S/4HANA Public: Implementation Essentials, Finance Foundation, Finance Expansion (mit Services wie Advanced Financial Closing, Advanced Cash Operations, Group Reporting)
  • S/4HANA Private: Clean Core, Business AI CoE, Finance Optimization, SAP Methodology
  • Supply Chain/IBP: APO Transformation, Sales and Operations Planning, Demand Planning, Inventory Optimization
  • Ariba, SuccessFactors, CX, BTP, Business Data Cloud: Jeweils eigene Success Packs

Grafik aus dem Webinar: SuccessPack am Beispiel von CloudERP Public. 

Kunden müssen nicht alle Services eines Packs buchen. Die Packs dienen als thematische Orientierung und heben das Gespräch auf die Ebene von Prozessen und Geschäftsthemen statt einzelner Service-Tickets. 

Business AI: Strukturierte Begleitung über alle Stufen

Das Thema KI zog sich als roter Faden durch den Vortrag. Jesper Waaben zeigte, wie die drei Plan-Stufen beim AI-Rollout ineinandergreifen:

  • Foundational: Erste Guidance-Services im Self-Service-Format
  • Advanced: Expert Advisory zu Governance, Compliance und Clean Core für KI. Workshops zur Identifikation von KI-Use-Cases. Aktivierung von Joule, Agents und intelligenten Apps. AI Business Readiness Workshops, um die Organisation vorzubereiten
  • Max: Umsetzung identifizierter Use Cases in Prototypen und Individualentwicklungen

Die Services vom Advanced & Max Plan lassen sich einzeln buchen oder als durchgängige Begleitung auf der KI-Reise nutzen. 

Darstellungen aus dem Webinar die zeigt, wie die Success Pläne bei der AI Adoption helfen. 

Marktvalidierung: IDC und Forrester bestätigen den Ansatz

Zwei unabhängige Analysen von IDC und Forrester – durchgeführt mit Fokus auf Preferred Success aber durchaus vergleichbar – bestätigten drei zentrale Erkenntnisse:

  1. Zugang zu SAP-Expertise ist geschäftskritisch – bei den hohen Investitionen in SAP-Systeme brauchen Kunden direkten Zugang zu Herstellerwissen
  2. Geschäftsanwender erwarten schnelleren Nutzen aus IT-Investitionen, was den Druck auf IT-Abteilungen erhöht
  3. KI verändert Softwarelandschaft und Investitionshorizonte radikal – Kunden suchen gezielt Unterstützung bei der Einordnung

Kunden mit Success Plans kommen laut den Analysen schneller und sicherer ins Ziel mit ihren Projekten. Details zu den Studien finden sich hier auf der sap.com Seite zu den Advanced Success Plans. 

Aussagen aus dem Webinar

Anbei einige Aussagen von Jesper, die ich pointiert fand.

„Früher durften wir mit Preferred Success nicht in die Systeme reingehen. Jetzt dürfen wir mit Hands-on Activation neue Funktionalitäten aktivieren und eine leichte Konfiguration anleiten."

„Mit den Success Plans kriegen Sie SAP an die Hüfte geschnürt – und Sie können, wenn Sie wollen, einfach von der Hüfte einen Experten hochziehen."

„Ich habe in den letzten Jahren sehr oft gesehen, dass wir Services pushen, um einfach Services zu pushen – aber nicht notwendigerweise die richtigen. Diese Orchestrierung ist nicht zu unterschätzen."

„Wir können uns nicht leisten, KI nicht anzugehen. Und da kann man die Success Plans als Unterstützung und Begleitung dafür benutzen."

„Wir haben schon in den ersten zwei Monaten mehr als 200 Kunden, die Advanced Success Plans gekauft haben."

Praktische Tipps für die Umsetzung

  • Cloud ALM aktiv nutzen: Die KI-gestützte Guidance setzt voraus, dass Cloud ALM im Einsatz ist. Wer hier früh investiert, profitiert stärker von den analytischen Services.
  • Success Packs als Einstieg nutzen: Statt einzelne Services aus dem Katalog zu wählen, bieten die thematischen Bündelungen eine bessere Orientierung – etwa „Clean Core" oder „Finance Expansion".
  • Orchestrierung ernst nehmen: Nutzen Sie den Customer Success Manager aktiv, um die richtigen Services zur richtigen Zeit zu identifizieren. Nicht die Menge der gebuchten Services zählt, sondern deren Passung.
  • Hands-on Activation mit dem Projektteam abstimmen: Die neue Möglichkeit der direkten Systemaktivierung ist wertvoll, sollte aber immer in Abstimmung mit internen Teams und Implementierungspartnern erfolgen.
  • KI-Readiness als Einstiegsthema wählen: Die AI Business Readiness Workshops im Advanced Plan helfen, die Organisation vorzubereiten, bevor konkrete Use Cases umgesetzt werden.
  • Value Management von Anfang an einsetzen: Die Baseline für Wertverfolgung früh zu legen, macht den Nutzen der Success Plans messbar und argumentierbar.

Resümee

Der Advanced Success Plan ist die konsequente Weiterentwicklung von Preferred Success – mit dem entscheidenden Unterschied, dass SAP-Experten jetzt direkt im System arbeiten dürfen. Die Einführung von Success Packs als thematische Bündelungen adressiert das Komplexitätsproblem eines wachsenden Service-Portfolios. Mit bereits über 200 Kunden in den ersten zwei Monaten zeigt sich, dass der Markt das neue Modell annimmt.

Die durchgängige KI-Integration über alle Plan-Stufen hinweg positioniert die Success Plans als zentralen Baustein für Unternehmen, die ihre SAP-Landschaft zukunftsfähig aufstellen wollen. Jesper hat exemplarisch schön gezeigt, wo der Advanced Success Plan einen Unterschied macht - auch bei AI. 

Was mich persönlich freut: Das Thema Lernen und Adoption wird nicht als Nebenschauplatz behandelt, sondern ist strukturell in die Success Plans eingebaut – von den Learning Journeys im Foundational Plan bis zur AI Business Readiness im Advanced Plan. 

Wo finden sich weiterführende Informationen zu den Success Plänen? Im Überblicks Blog sammeln wir Links und Informationen: SAP Success Pläne - Der Guide zum neuen SAP Support und Service Modell 2026

Zusätzlich ist am 25.06. ein Vor-Ort Event geplant in Walldorf – Details finden Sie hier.

FAQ

Was unterscheidet den Advanced Success Plan von Preferred Success?

Der Advanced Plan baut auf Preferred Success auf, bringt aber wesentliche Erweiterungen und Neuerungen: Hands-on Activation erlaubt SAP-Experten erstmals, direkt in Kundensystemen Funktionalitäten zu aktivieren und leichte Konfigurationen anzuleiten. Hinzu kommen KI-gestützte Guidance über Cloud ALM, verbesserte SLAs und eine breitere Abdeckung der gesamten Business Suite. Interessant ist auch zu wissen, dass Elemente von Preferred Success Expanded Edition beim Advanced Plan dabei sind - was früher eine noch teurere Edition war. Damit bekommen Kunden mehr für weniger Kosten. 

Gibt es eine maximale Anzahl an Beratungstagen oder Tickets im Advanced Plan?

Nein. Die Anzahl der Services im Advanced Plan ist nicht limitiert. Kunden können so oft und so viele Services buchen, wie sie benötigen.  

Was sind Success Packs?

Success Packs sind thematische Bündelungen zusammengehöriger Services – etwa „Clean Core", „Finance Expansion" oder „Demand Planning". Sie dienen als Orientierungshilfe und heben das Gespräch von einzelnen Service-Tickets auf die Ebene von Geschäftsthemen und Prozessen. Kunden müssen nicht alle enthaltenen Services buchen.

Welche Rolle spielt der Implementierungspartner im Success-Plan-Konzept?

SAP tritt mit den Success Plans nicht in Konkurrenz zu Implementierungspartnern. Die Hands-on Activation muss mit dem Projektteam und gegebenenfalls dem Partner abgestimmt werden. SAP liefert Expertise zu Best Practices, Standards und Architektur – Implementierung kann beim Partner bleiben.

Muss ich Cloud ALM nutzen, um vom Advanced Plan zu profitieren?

Für die volle Nutzung der KI-gestützten Guidance ist Cloud ALM die Voraussetzung. Die übrigen Services – Activation, Adoption Guidance, Expert Advisory – sind davon unabhängig nutzbar.

Welche Experten liefern die Advanced-Services?

Die eingesetzten Experten sind erfahrene SAP-Berater aus dem Projektgeschäft und erfahrene Customer Success Experten, die Services für Advanced und teilweise Max liefern – keine reine Support-Organisation.

Für welche SAP-Lösungen gibt es Service Maps?

Service Maps existieren für S/4HANA Public, S/4HANA Private, Supply Chain/IBP, Ariba, SuccessFactors, Customer Experience (Sales & Service Cloud, Commerce Cloud, Marketing Cloud), BTP und Business Data Cloud.

Wie unterstützt der Advanced Plan konkret bei Business AI?

Im Advanced Plan stehen Expert Advisory zu KI-Governance und Compliance, Workshops zur Identifikation von KI-Use-Cases, AI Business Readiness Workshops sowie die Aktivierung von Joule, Agents und intelligenten Apps zur Verfügung. Er unterstützt somit entlang jeder Stufe Ihrer AI Adoption. Für die Umsetzung identifizierter Use Cases in Prototypen ist der Max Plan erforderlich.

Gibt es bereits Erfahrungswerte aus dem Markt?

Ja. In den ersten zwei Monaten nach Launch haben bereits mehr als 200 Kunden den Advanced Success Plan gekauft. Unabhängige Analysen von IDC und Forrester bestätigen, dass Kunden mit Success Plans schneller und sicherer ihre Projektziele erreichen.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2: Final What's New & Product Assistance available
entry.content:
With immediate effect, the final What's New information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2 is now available.

To access the final What’s New information, please use the What’s New Viewer. In addition, the final Product Assistance (aka Application Help) for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2 is available. Please use the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Help Portal page to access it.

For you as a customer, we recommend to also use the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool. The Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool provides you with a personalized and tailored view on What’s New information based on the activated and used scope within your system. The information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2 will be available in RASD within the next few days. A valid Customer S-user ID is required for login. We recommend to use RASD to prepare for upgrades and updates of your system and to focus on the new and changed features that are relevant for you as a customer.

You can use the commenting feature for What’s New. With this feature, authors can evaluate your feedback and incorporate it into the documentation, if needed. We hope that by opening this channel of communication, we can learn how to further improve our What’s New content for you. To use this feature, you need to be logged into the Help Portal. The feature will only be available in the English version of the documents. To learn more about how the feedback process works in SAP Help Portal, see Giving Documentation Feedback.

entry.title: Third-Party Payments with SIPO (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)
entry.content:
This blog post introduces business scenarios and explains how to process payment with SIPO in Czech Republic for SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition.  

SIPO (Soustředěné Inkaso Plateb Obyvatelstva) that is used for direct debit of multiple payments through the Czech Post’s system. It is a national payment aggregation system operated by the Czech Post. This business concept relates to payments through a third party. 

Business Scenarios 

Let’s start with the parties involved in SIPO payments.   

  1. Customers that have SIPO connection number pay on a regular basis  
  2. Service providers that offer services of utilities, multimedia, or internet, send payment requests to SIPO  
  3. Czech Post Office, or the SIPO operator, collects all payment requests, aggregates them into one payment, and distribute paid money by customers to service providers  
  4. Banks that serve as payment channels carry out direct debit from bank accounts 

There are two business scenarios of payments involving SIPO:  

Scenario 1: You, as a service provider, post receivable items. After executing a payment run, you send a SIPO file, as an outgoing file, to the Czech Post for informing the customers to pay.   

Scenario 2: Continuing with scenario 1, you, as a service provider, want to clear the open items by processing incoming SIPO files after your customer completes the payment. 

Prerequisites 

Bank Account Settings   

Since the payments with SIPO order are not directly paid out from the payer's bank account, instead it is paid out from the post office, it is necessary to create a fictive bank account and account ID for the post office.   

Open the Manage Bank Accounts app.   

For your company code, create a fictive Bank Account and Account ID for the Czech Post.

Define Parameters for Paying Out    

Since payment is carried out through the third party (Czech Post),  you need to identify which payee whom the post office pays.    

In the Define Parameters for Foreign Payment Transactions (ID: 101953) configuration to fill the service provider's SIPO number in the Customer number field.  

Define Payment Methods  

Open the Define Payment Methods (ID: 101952) to create a payment method or use the pre-delivered format - CZ_PSTLPAYMENT_SIPO as the payment medium format for Czech SIPO payments.

Define Payment Lot Specifications   

Open the Define Payment Lot Specification activity (ID:107255).   

Select the format type Czech Republic: Postal Payment Item (SIPO file) (CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-ZA) and complete the Bank statement settings section.   
Note: It is not required to configure the Czech Republic: Postal Payment Header (SIPO file) (CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-PZ).

Define Interpretation Rules for Note to Payee    

Open the Define Interpretation Rules for Note to Payee activity (ID: 103949) to assign keywords.

Procedure

Processing SIPO Outgoing Payment Files 

  1. You post contract accounting receivable items.  
  2. Execute a payment run. The payment order then is created.  
  3. During the payment run, the system uses the payment format CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-SIPO to generate the payment file for the Czech Post.  
  4. You then send the payment file to Czech Post.  
    Czech Post informs the customer to conduct the payment.  

Processing SIPO Incoming Payment Files

After the customer pays, the Czech Post sends back the SIPO files.   

The SIPO files contain the following files:  

  • SIPO header file – Includes the total settlement amount that matches the bank statement of the incoming payment  
  • SIPO item file – lists the clearing of open receivable items per customer’s individual SIPO number 

You have to import both files in the Manage Incoming Payment File (3rd-Party Payments) app (F1680).   

  1. Open the Manage Incoming Payment File (3rd-Party Payments) app (F1680). 
    • Select Import and choose Third-party Payment.
    • Select Czech Republic: Postal Payment Header (SIPO file) and upload the SIPO header file.  
    • Repeat the same operation and upload the Czech Republic: Postal Payment Item (SIPO file).  
  2. Open the Process Imported Third-Party Payments app (F9000) to schedule a run. 
    • Select Third-Party Payment: Transfer data as the job template.   
    • In the Parameters, select Czech Republic: Postal Payment Item (SIPO file) (CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-ZA) as a format type.   
      Note: When processing the imported file, you only need to process the item files. (Header files are not required.)  
    • Schedule a run and you can see that the payment lot number is created in the log .
      If you select the Post Lot check box and you can directly process the payment order lot without additional operation.  
  3. Run the payment lot and you clear the open items. Use the payment lot and process it in the Process Payment (Order) Lot app and clear open items of the customer. If you did not select the checkbox in the previous step. 

Is this blog post useful to you? Leave your comments below to let us know your thoughts.

entry.title: New SAP Signavio Value Accelerators: Process Landscape Design & Fit-to-Standard in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
We are excited to announce two new additions to the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library:

  • Process Landscape Design & Fit-to-Standard in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • Process Landscape Design & Fit-to-Standard in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

These accelerators bring a harmonized process structure that connects the SAP Reference Business Process Architecture with SAP S/4HANA best practices ready to use out of the box. They are built for everyone working on transformation, running Fit-to-Standard workshops, or who’s simply looking to enrich their Signavio workspace with first process content to get started.

Key highlights include:

  • SAP Reference Business Process Architecture including specific end-to-end variants
  • Out-of-the-box synchronization with SAP LeanIX thanks to renewed structure
  • Mapping to S/4HANA best practice diagrams
  • Dictionary entries to capture information structurally

You can find both accelerators directly in your SAP Signavio workspace by opening the Value Accelerator Library (VAL) and searching for "Process Landscape Design".

We have put together more information and a step-by-step installation and setup guide. Check it out here.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

If you have any questions, feel free to reach out to your BTM contact.

entry.title: Which Business AI solutions are available within my SAP Cloud Subscription?
entry.content:
In the last couple of months, I have been talking to many SAP customers looking to implement AI initiatives in their companies to improve productivity gains and/or because their management is asking about AI in their current business processes.

At the same time, generative AI models have made it easy for a set of SAP business end users to start prompting in natural language and create supplemental ideas for their day-to-day work, adding speed and pressure of AI to their IT counterparts or simply ignoring IT and creating their own tool domains.

However, we all know that while the accessibility of AI tools to SAP business end users is proliferating for idea creation, creating ideas via prompts doesn’t address all the companies’ fundamental IT policies and security. In the scope of the user, they might see local solutions to help them improve, but depending on the IT policies, they don’t know if the SAP business end users are aware that some ideas are creating potential challenges in security, data harmonization, risks, and costs.

In SAP, we built our SAP Business AI based on three core principles: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible.

Relevant: AI is embedded directly into SAP's leading enterprise applications and business processes, providing contextually appropriate capabilities where users work daily.

Reliable: AI is grounded in unique business data and business process context, ensuring accurate, trustworthy outputs based on the most important enterprise data with intact business context.

Responsible: AI is delivered with the highest levels of concern for security, privacy, compliance, and ethics, maintaining human oversight and agency while addressing bias and ensuring transparency.

I know these core principles might sound philosophical, but they were designed to help our customers implement SAP Business AI without adding risks to their business. So why not look at anything SAP delivered out of the box first, which was built with these core principles in mind to protect your current investments.

So in this blog, I would like to expose you to where to look at all available capabilities and understand if these capabilities are included in your current Cloud Subscription or not, and also understand how you can move forward with them. Now, I recognize there are many blogs on some of the content already available, which I also recommend you read, e.g. SAP Business AI Catalog and AI Estimator in SAP Discovery Center , Unlock Base AI Package for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – Enjoy Joule and Embedded AI Features for Free, etc. However, my focus here is exploring AI use cases, understanding entitlements, and deciding how to move quickly to explore.

To run this case, let’s say you are an SAP Business Suite customer running a couple of SAP Public Cloud Solutions productively, including: SAP Cloud ERP (also known as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition), SAP Integrated Business Planning, Concur Expense, and Concur Travel. Now you want to explore what SAP provides for artificial intelligence included in your cloud subscription.

So first you need to go to the SAP Discovery Center: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/ai-catalog/

SAP Discovery Center.

You can log in to your account if you have one. In the filter section, you will look for the commercial Types : Premium and Base.

Premium: Premium AI includes advanced and specialized AI services powered by AI Units. It enables more complex and scalable use cases such as Joule Premium and Document Grounding, helping organizations extend AI into higher-value scenarios across departments and workflows. In general, if you have not subscribed to these capabilities before, you will be able to add them to your contract with an additional pricing item.

Base: Base AI provides the foundational AI capabilities included in all SAP Cloud applications. These everyday AI features support core business processes—for example, generating insights, recommendations, or automating simple tasks across SAP solutions. Base AI is included in standard cloud subscriptions and can be used without limits or additional cost. So, in other words, you have the rights to use these capabilities as part of your SAP Cloud Subscription. You might need to add a subscription (e.g., Joule Base); however, this subscription comes at no additional cost.

So let's look at all Base capabilities for the system landscape (we are filtering the Discovery Center based on the date of release of this blog, so be aware more capabilities might have been included).

For SAP Cloud ERP:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP and Base commercial type.

For SAP Integrated Business Planning:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP and Base commercial type.

For SAP Concur Travel and Concur Expense:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by Concur Travel and Concur Expense and Base commercial type.

So, putting it all together for our customer example (SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, and Concur Travel and Expense), you have almost 40 use cases where you can enable AI within your current system landscape, with entitlements already included in your cloud subscriptions.

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, Concur Travel and Concur Expense and Base commercial type.

So as indicated, you now have plenty of cases that you can explore with your SAP Business end users that were developed with the core principles that I discussed before: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible.

Now, when you start exploring the AI capabilities, we have included all resources for you to see what is included, benefits, business value, resources for activation, other related products, and roadmaps.

This is important to review in order to provide your SAP Business end users with a clear explanation of what this capability will bring, along with the fact that since you are running a public cloud solution, you are technically ready to consume the capability out of the box or by just executing an activation.

SAP Discovery Center – Details on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edion, Joule (Base)

Agentic AI

As all industries are moving more toward autonomous agent capabilities to help drive the business, you can also use the Discovery Center to review what SAP has in store. Before you start with development of your own agent-specific capabilities, as indicated above, SAP Agents are also built with the core principles: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible. So, before you embark on a development cycle of agentic capabilities using other tools in your landscape, I advise you to see what SAP offers out of the box.

SAP Discovery Center – AI Agents for SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, Concur Travel & Concur Expense (Base & Premium).

Keep in mind that SAP Business AI capabilities are expanding quickly, so I recommend you check the Discovery Center regularly.

If you are an SAP Cloud ERP customer, I also suggest you look at this blog as it also presents some principles on AI that are relevant: Foundational Elements for Harnessing the Power of Business AI in Your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Ed.

I encourage you to discuss these points with your SAP Customer Success Manager, SAP Account Executive, or SAP Partner. They may be able to provide you with additional recommendations, guidance, and help you discover new ideas on SAP Business AI capabilities that can help you bring more business outcomes.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @Marco_Valencia (SAP Business Suite Customer Success Management).

entry.title: Missing Simulation in Workflow Configuration: Why PRs Deserve the Same Transparency as POs
entry.content:
With SAP S/4HANA release 2602, SAP introduced a highly welcome enhancement: the ability to simulate workflows for Purchase Orders (POs) in the app Manage Workflows for Purchase Orders. This new functionality provides much‑needed transparency by allowing users to see which workflow will be triggered for a given document and why.

 

However, when looking at the overall procurement process, one question naturally arises:

Why does this simulation capability stop at Purchase Orders?

Purchase Requisitions (PRs) are the starting point of the procurement lifecycle, yet the app Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions (F2705A) still lacks any comparable workflow simulation functionality. This blog post outlines why this distinction is problematic and why extending workflow simulation to Purchase Requisitions would be a meaningful and consistent improvement.

The Current Challenge: Limited Transparency in PR Workflow Determination

Workflow configuration for Purchase Requisitions is often complex:

  • Multiple workflow definitions may exist
  • Start conditions overlap
  • Different approval paths apply depending on value, material group, account assignment, or requester

Today, when a Purchase Requisition is created, the system determines the workflow in the background. If an unexpected workflow is triggered—or none at all—users and administrators are left with limited options:

  • Trial-and-error testing
  • Manual analysis of start conditions
  • Debugging or log interpretation

In contrast, the new workflow simulation for Purchase Orders allows users to proactively validate workflow behavior before real documents are processed.

This creates an inconsistency in tooling between two tightly related business objects.

Why Workflow Simulation Matters—Especially for Purchase Requisitions

Purchase Requisitions are not just another document type. They are:

  • The entry point of procurement
  • Often created by non-procurement users
  • The basis for downstream Purchase Orders and contracts

Introducing workflow simulation for PRs would address several real-world challenges:

1. Validation of Workflow Setup

Simulation would make it immediately clear:

  • Which workflow is selected
  • Which start conditions are fulfilled
  • Why one workflow is chosen over another

This is particularly valuable in systems where multiple workflows coexist.

2. Prevention of Incorrect Approvals

Without simulation, misconfigured workflows may only be discovered after release:

  • Wrong approvers
  • Missing approvals
  • Delays due to rework

Simulation enables preventive validation instead of reactive correction.

3. Reduced Dependency on Technical Analysis

Today, resolving workflow issues often requires:

  • Technical expertise
  • Backend analysis
  • IT involvement

A simulation feature empowers business users—especially purchasers—to validate workflows on their own.

Proposed Improvement

Extend the workflow simulation functionality introduced in release 2602 for Purchase Orders also to:

  • App: Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions
  • Fiori App ID: F2705A
  • Business Role: BR_PURCHASER

The simulation should:

  • Accept PR-relevant input parameters
  • Display the selected workflow
  • Show which start conditions are met
  • Clearly indicate why other workflows are not selected

In short: the same transparency that is now available for Purchase Orders should also be available for Purchase Requisitions.

Business Value and Benefits

For purchasing organizations, this enhancement would deliver tangible value:

✔️  Faster and safer workflow configuration

✔️  Higher process reliability

✔️  Fewer approval errors

✔️  Less troubleshooting effort

✔️  Better user confidence in workflow logic

Most importantly, it would ensure process consistency across the procurement lifecycle.

Call to Action: Help Improve Workflow Configuration

The introduction of workflow simulation for Purchase Orders in SAP S/4HANA 2602 is a strong step in the right direction. But by stopping at Purchase Orders, SAP leaves a critical gap at the very start of procurement.

If transparency and predictability are important for workflow configuration—and they are—then Purchase Requisitions deserve the same level of simulation support.

And if you have read this far, I encourage you to:

👉  Please Vote to Idea # 365219 Workflow Simulation for Purchase Requisitions on SAP Influence and help move this enhancement forward.

References

  • Workflow Simulation for Purchase Orders | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026: Execute CX as your Growth Stratetegy
entry.content:
Customer experience has entered a new era. In 2026, engagement alone is no longer enough. Businesses win by executing with intelligence, speed, and context across the entire enterprise. With AI embedded directly into sales, service, commerce, and engagement workflows, SAP Customer Experience is transforming CX from a front-office function into a true growth engine of your company. In this new Product Inspiration Series, we explore how SAP CX brings executional AI into the flow of work by connecting customer signals to real outcomes, powered by the business data and processes that run your organization.
The special character of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series remains unchanged. Each video:
  • spotlights concrete innovations across the SAP CX portfolio
  • is created by product and industry experts, for fellow experts
  • opens with a short Q&A to set the context—the why, what, and how
  • follows a zero‑slides approach, focusing entirely on the product itself
  • delivers in‑depth explorations and hands‑on demos
  • helps you better understand the Consumer Industries and SAP CX product strategy
